Satellite-based Land-Cover Classificatio: This domain covers deep learning approaches for classifying land cover and land use from multispectral, hyperspectral, and high-resolution satellite and UAV imagery, spanning objects and ecosystems such as aquaculture ponds, mangroves, forests, farmlands, and urban greenspace. It emphasizes methodological challenges including data augmentation, dataset creation for semantic segmentation, self-supervised and multiple-instance learning, and assessing data limitations. Case studies range from region-specific datasets like IndiaSat to greenspace quantification in Karachi and LULC change mapping in Rwanda.
Remote Sensing for Deforestation & Fores: This domain focuses on detecting and measuring forest and land change over time using multi-temporal satellite and radar imagery, including reviews of Earth observation methods in the tropics and applications like selective logging and reforestation detection. It features deep learning and time-series techniques such as BFAST, echo state networks, cascade frameworks, and the Segment Anything Model for change detection. Applications extend from the Brazilian Amazon and Kenya to fine-resolution vegetation monitoring and detection of destroyed settlements in Nigeria.
Forest Carbon & Biomass Estimation via R: This domain covers quantifying forest carbon stocks, fluxes, aboveground biomass, and canopy height using remote sensing tools such as GEDI laser ranging, Landsat, and SAR combined with machine learning. Topics include global carbon flux mapping, uncertainty-aware estimation via mixture density networks, geospatial foundation models, and carbon sequestration assessment including urban forests and tree age discrimination. It also incorporates sensing hardware like the Fluxbot CO2 flux chamber and broader reviews of AI in sustainable forestry.
Carbon Offset & REDD+ Integrity: This domain examines the credibility and accuracy of forest carbon offset and REDD+ programs, highlighting problems such as systematic over-crediting in California's program and overstated emission reductions from voluntary REDD+ projects in the Brazilian Amazon. It also addresses machine learning benchmarks for equitable monitoring, reporting, and verification of nature-based solutions.
Community-Based Forest Governance, Tenur: This domain addresses community management of forests through the lens of land tenure, payment for environmental services, and participatory approaches to mapping and governance. It includes deep learning applications for transparent PES communication, machine learning for coviability transition pathways, and participatory mapping combined with satellite imagery, as illustrated by cases in India and the Chunati Wildlife Sanctuary.
Community Climate Vulnerability & Adapta: This domain covers tools and methods for assessing community vulnerability and resilience to climate change, emphasizing participatory and community-driven evaluation processes such as the CoDriVE-Programme Designer and Commons Connect application. It includes natural resource management consultation, retrospective resilience assessment of semi-arid farming systems in India, geospatial and AI-based flood and deforestation vulnerability mapping, and co-designing microclimate sensors with communities.
Public Works & Social Protection as Carb: This domain explores the intersection of India's MGNREGA social protection and public works programs with climate mitigation, focusing on the carbon sequestration co-benefits achieved and potentially achievable through such schemes. It also examines pro-poor climate policy innovations, small-scale infrastructure, and land-use changes around mining sites in India.
Nature-Based Solutions & Ecosystem Valua: This domain covers the valuation and planning of nature-based solutions and ecosystems, including causal frameworks for large-scale ecosystem valuation such as wetland benefits for flood mitigation. It incorporates landscape ecology metrics in sustainability planning, machine learning methods like adaptive Dice loss for wetland delineation and deep Gaussian processes for mitigation decision support, alongside critical assessment of the value and limits of nature-based solutions.
Biodiversity & Conservation Planning Opt: This domain focuses on optimization approaches for conservation and biodiversity planning, including derivative-free optimization frameworks for land conservation decisions and optimizing trees for carbon sequestration. It also addresses constraint-based spatial planning to improve graph connectivity in pursuit of the 30-by-30 Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework target.
Deforestation Detection & Driver Classif: This domain covers detecting deforestation and classifying its underlying drivers from satellite imagery using deep learning, including vision transformers (ForestViT), rotation-equivariant segmentation, and interpretable and lightweight attribution models. Applications include forecasting deforestation in the Amazon, tracking illegal cattle ranching, classifying deforestation drivers in Indonesia via ForestNet, and analyzing climate risks to soy-maize cropping from Amazon deforestation.
Forest Carbon Stock Estimation & Carbon: This domain focuses on quantifying forest carbon and ensuring the reliability of carbon credits, using approaches like Bayesian state-space models for deforestation baselines and deep learning with aerial imagery to address overestimation of forest carbon. It also covers AI-driven methods for automating carbon sequestration site assessment and joint industry-university efforts to tackle carbon storage challenges. Collectively, the readings aim to improve the accuracy and trustworthiness of forest carbon accounting and credit markets.
Individual Tree & Canopy Structure Measu: This domain covers techniques for measuring tree-level and canopy attributes, including species classification from LiDAR, satellite, and 3D tomographic SAR data, DBH estimation from depth maps, and canopy height and cover mapping at scales from Chicago to India. It also encompasses segmenting dead trees, reconstructing tree structure, assessing urban forest health from street view imagery, and geographically localizing natural objects like palms. Together the readings emphasize fine-grained detection, classification, and structural measurement of trees using diverse remote and ground-level sensing.
Foundation Models & Data-Efficient Learn: This domain explores large-scale pretrained and multi-modal foundation models for remote sensing, including forest monitoring models (FoMo), climatic and geographic location encoders, and masked autoencoders for data reconstruction. It also addresses data-efficient strategies such as visual prompt-tuning, few-shot instance segmentation, weakly-semi-supervised detection, data programming for labeling, and fine-tuning language models for biodiversity entity recognition. The readings collectively aim to reduce labeled-data requirements and build general-purpose models for ecological and Earth observation tasks.
Vegetation & Ecosystem Dynamics Forecast: This domain concerns modeling and forecasting vegetation and ecosystem behavior over time, including forest greenness monitoring, phenology prediction, net primary productivity under warming, and drought resilience analysis via Shapley values. It spans data-driven and mechanistic approaches, hybrid and physics-encoded spatiotemporal deep learning, and datasets/challenges like EarthNet2021 for forecasting localized climate impacts. The readings emphasize predicting how vegetation and land surfaces respond to climatic drivers across regions such as Africa and India.
Community Co-Design & Indigenous Knowled: This domain centers on incorporating community perspectives and Indigenous knowledge into climate monitoring and resilience efforts. The reading focuses on capturing and digitizing Indigenous knowledge to strengthen community resilience to climate change, highlighting participatory and co-design approaches to climate data.
Interpretability & Verification for Clim: This domain addresses making machine learning for climate finance and forest conservation transparent, trustworthy, and verifiable, as in GainForest's interpretable models on satellite imagery and Xingu's explanations of geospatial predictions under weak supervision. It also covers verification methods like TrueBranch's metric-learning-based checks on conservation projects and systems designed to be past- and future-aware when evaluating climate policies. Together the readings emphasize interpretability, accountability, and validation to support credible climate finance decisions.
